title: Introduction to unheadr
text: functions unheadr help us rework data shared people, human-readable structure tidier machine-readable structure can perform common data manipulation tasks.

title: Introduction to unheadr
text: unheadr works tibble data frame objects suffer common issues : Embedded subheaders Broken values Merged cells values wrapped within columns Broken rows Broken headers Embedded subheaders usually grouping variables embedded another variable, used show hierarchical data create small multiples data. simple example coffee shop menu: beverage type embedded ‘drinks’ variable. can match regular expressions, can move grouping values variable using untangle2() Broken values usually happen ’re pressed space. whatever reason, entries Barcelona 1992 London 2012 Olympics broken across two contiguous rows NAs used padding variables. case, can use unbreak_vals() ‘unbreak’ lines ‘Games’ variable, matching strings start numbers. Wrapped columns often happen merge cells spreadsheets use table formatting word processor, resulting empty NA values used pad vertical space. can unwrap ‘teams’ values single string using unrwap_cols(). less opposite separate_rows() tidyr. Line breaks meant separate values inside merged cell can problematic. often results parsing tables PDF Word documents. remove duplicated trailing new line sequences “\n”, can use squish_newlines(). example, applying function across variables problematic line breaks possible separate rows tidy structure. Broken rows values two contiguous rows broken padded empty NA values. case, can match value variable along row broken values. Broken headers variable names broken across first rows. , column names broken. top three rows (addition column name) contain fragments name mashed together column-wise. mash_colnames() function makes many header rows column names. names broken across top three rows, goes n_name_rows argument. Unlike functions unheadr, provide number rows directly, rather attempt string matching. importing data broken headers R, variable names always assigned values top row, leaving us automatically generated names (e.g. X1, X2, X3, etc.). case, keep_names argument mash_colnames() lets us ignore object names building new names first four rows data. Lastly, sliding_headers argument mash_colnames can used tables ragged names, every column value first row. cases, attribution adjacency assumed, sliding_headers = TRUE names filled row-wise. can useful tables reporting survey data experimental designs untidy manner.
code:
dat <- data.frame(   drink = c(     "Cold Drinks", "Soda", "Water", "Juice", "Lemonade",     "Hot Drinks", "Tea", "Coffee"   ),   price = c(NA, 2.99, 1.99, 3.15, 2, NA, 3.99, 1.99), stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  dat #>         drink price #> 1 Cold Drinks    NA #> 2        Soda  2.99 #> 3       Water  1.99 #> 4       Juice  3.15 #> 5    Lemonade  2.00 #> 6  Hot Drinks    NA #> 7         Tea  3.99 #> 8      Coffee  1.99 untangle2(dat, "Drinks$", drink, "beverage_type") #> 2 matches #>      drink price beverage_type #> 1     Soda  2.99   Cold Drinks #> 2    Water  1.99   Cold Drinks #> 3    Juice  3.15   Cold Drinks #> 4 Lemonade  2.00   Cold Drinks #> 5      Tea  3.99    Hot Drinks #> 6   Coffee  1.99    Hot Drinks OGames <- tibble(   Games = c("Los Angeles 1984", "Barcelona", "1992", "Atlanta 1996", "Sydney 2000", "London", "2012"),   Country = c("USA", "Spain", NA, "USA", "Australia", "UK", NA),   Soccer_gold_medal = c("France", "Spain", NA, "Nigeria", "Cameroon", "Mexico", NA) ) OGames #> # A tibble: 7 × 3 #>   Games            Country   Soccer_gold_medal #>   <chr>            <chr>     <chr>             #> 1 Los Angeles 1984 USA       France            #> 2 Barcelona        Spain     Spain             #> 3 1992             NA        NA                #> 4 Atlanta 1996     USA       Nigeria           #> 5 Sydney 2000      Australia Cameroon          #> 6 London           UK        Mexico            #> 7 2012             NA        NA OGames %>%   unbreak_vals("^[0-9]", Games, Games_unbroken, slice_groups = TRUE) %>%   select(Games_unbroken, everything()) #> Warning: argument slice_groups is deprecated; extra rows and the variable with #> broken values are now dropped by default. #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   Games_unbroken   Country   Soccer_gold_medal #>   <chr>            <chr>     <chr>             #> 1 Los Angeles 1984 USA       France            #> 2 Barcelona 1992   Spain     Spain             #> 3 Atlanta 1996     USA       Nigeria           #> 4 Sydney 2000      Australia Cameroon          #> 5 London 2012      UK        Mexico knicks <- data.frame(   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,   player = c("Allan Houston", NA, "Latrell Sprewell", NA, NA),   teams = c(     "Pistons", "Knicks", "Warriors", "Knicks",     "Timberwolves"   ),   position = c("Shooting guard", NA, "Small forward", NA, NA) ) knicks #>             player        teams       position #> 1    Allan Houston      Pistons Shooting guard #> 2             <NA>       Knicks           <NA> #> 3 Latrell Sprewell     Warriors  Small forward #> 4             <NA>       Knicks           <NA> #> 5             <NA> Timberwolves           <NA> knicks %>% unwrap_cols(groupingVar = player, separator = ", ") #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   player           teams                          position       #>   <chr>            <chr>                          <chr>          #> 1 Allan Houston    Pistons, Knicks                Shooting guard #> 2 Latrell Sprewell Warriors, Knicks, Timberwolves Small forward merged_dat <- data.frame(   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,   Name = c("Valery V."),   Asset = c("Apartment

") #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>   Name      Asset     Area  Status  #>   <chr>     <chr>     <chr> <chr>   #> 1 Valery V. Apartment 45.7  Owned   #> 2 Valery V. Plot      69.2  Owned   #> 3 Valery V. House     65.0  In use  #> 4 Valery V. Other     32.2  Unknown #> 5 Valery V. Apartment 36.9  Owned basketball <-   data.frame(     stringsAsFactors = FALSE,     v1 = c(       "Player", NA, "Sleve McDichael", "Dean Wesrey",       "Karl Dandleton", "Mike Sernandez",       "Glenallen Mixon", "Rey McSriff"     ),     v2 = c(       "Most points", "in a game", "55", "43", "41", "111", "109", "104"     ),     v3 = c(       "Season", "(year ending)", "2001", "2000", "2002",       "2000", "2002", "2001"     )   ) basketball #>                v1          v2            v3 #> 1          Player Most points        Season #> 2            <NA>   in a game (year ending) #> 3 Sleve McDichael          55          2001 #> 4     Dean Wesrey          43          2000 #> 5  Karl Dandleton          41          2002 #> 6  Mike Sernandez         111          2000 #> 7 Glenallen Mixon         109          2002 #> 8     Rey McSriff         104          2001 unbreak_rows(basketball, "^Most", v2) #> 1 match #>                v1                    v2                   v3 #> 1          Player Most points in a game Season (year ending) #> 2 Sleve McDichael                    55                 2001 #> 3     Dean Wesrey                    43                 2000 #> 4  Karl Dandleton                    41                 2002 #> 5  Mike Sernandez                   111                 2000 #> 6 Glenallen Mixon                   109                 2002 #> 7     Rey McSriff                   104                 2001 vehicles <-  data.frame(   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,            Vehicle = c(NA, NA, NA, "Truck", "Motorcycle", "Sedan", "Van"),              Price = c("in","2014",                        "(local currency)","50000","44000","33000","50000"),              Color = c(NA, NA, NA, "White", "Black", "Red", "White"),          Emissions = c("Certificate", NA, NA, "TRUE", "FALSE", "TRUE", "TRUE") ) vehicles #>      Vehicle            Price Color   Emissions #> 1       <NA>               in  <NA> Certificate #> 2       <NA>             2014  <NA>        <NA> #> 3       <NA> (local currency)  <NA>        <NA> #> 4      Truck            50000 White        TRUE #> 5 Motorcycle            44000 Black       FALSE #> 6      Sedan            33000   Red        TRUE #> 7        Van            50000 White        TRUE mash_colnames(df= vehicles, n_name_rows = 3, keep_names = TRUE) #>      Vehicle Price_in_2014_(local currency) Color Emissions_Certificate #> 4      Truck                          50000 White                  TRUE #> 5 Motorcycle                          44000 Black                 FALSE #> 6      Sedan                          33000   Red                  TRUE #> 7        Van                          50000 White                  TRUE vehicles_skip <-  data.frame(   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,                 X1 = c("Vehicle",NA,NA,NA,"Truck",                        "Motorcycle","Sedan","Van"),                 X2 = c("Price","in","2014",                        "(local currency)","50000","44000","33000","50000"),                 X3 = c("Color", NA, NA, NA, "White", "Black", "Red", "White"),                 X4 = c("Emissions","Certificate",NA,                        NA,"TRUE","FALSE","TRUE","TRUE") ) vehicles_skip #>           X1               X2    X3          X4 #> 1    Vehicle            Price Color   Emissions #> 2       <NA>               in  <NA> Certificate #> 3       <NA>             2014  <NA>        <NA> #> 4       <NA> (local currency)  <NA>        <NA> #> 5      Truck            50000 White        TRUE #> 6 Motorcycle            44000 Black       FALSE #> 7      Sedan            33000   Red        TRUE #> 8        Van            50000 White        TRUE mash_colnames(df= vehicles_skip, n_name_rows = 4, keep_names = FALSE) #>      Vehicle Price_in_2014_(local currency) Color Emissions_Certificate #> 5      Truck                          50000 White                  TRUE #> 6 Motorcycle                          44000 Black                 FALSE #> 7      Sedan                          33000   Red                  TRUE #> 8        Van                          50000 White                  TRUE survey <-  data.frame(   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,                 X1 = c("Participant", NA, "12", "34", "45", "123"),                 X2 = c("How did you hear about us?",                        "TV","TRUE","FALSE","FALSE","FALSE"),                 X3 = c(NA, "Social Media", "FALSE", "TRUE", "FALSE", "FALSE"),                 X4 = c(NA, "Radio", "FALSE", "TRUE", "FALSE", "TRUE"),                 X5 = c(NA, "Flyer", "FALSE", "FALSE", "FALSE", "FALSE"),                 X6 = c("Age", NA, "31", "23", "19", "24") )  survey #>            X1                         X2           X3    X4    X5   X6 #> 1 Participant How did you hear about us?         <NA>  <NA>  <NA>  Age #> 2        <NA>                         TV Social Media Radio Flyer <NA> #> 3          12                       TRUE        FALSE FALSE FALSE   31 #> 4          34                      FALSE         TRUE  TRUE FALSE   23 #> 5          45                      FALSE        FALSE FALSE FALSE   19 #> 6         123                      FALSE        FALSE  TRUE FALSE   24 mash_colnames(survey,2,keep_names = FALSE,sliding_headers = TRUE, sep = "_") #>   Participant How did you hear about us?_TV #> 3          12                          TRUE #> 4          34                         FALSE #> 5          45                         FALSE #> 6         123 title: Introduction to unheadr
text: bold text colored text italic text, text strikethrough underlined text double underlined text cell highlighting One example files bundled unheadr looks like :  Font formatting cell highlighting used label embedded grouping variable (meaningful formatting). annotate_mf() function flattens formatting character string describing formatting. hex8 code colors used cell text highlighting also included. apply approach cells spreadsheet, call annotate_mf_all(). bundled example file, negative values (first quarter losses) indicated bold.
code: example_spreadsheet <- system.file("extdata/dog_test.xlsx", package = "unheadr") annotate_mf(example_spreadsheet, orig = Task, new = Task_annotated) #> # A tibble: 11 × 3 #>    Task                                               Task_annotated       Score #>    <chr>                                              <chr>                <dbl> #>  1 Outdoor activities                                 (bold, highlight-FF…  7.67 #>  2 Walks on a loose leash without pulling             Walks on a loose le…  7    #>  3 Walks without chasing bicycles, animals, etc.      Walks without chasi…  6    #>  4 Greets friends and strangers without jumping       Greets friends and … 10    #>  5 Home behavior                                      (bold, highlight-FF…  8.5  #>  6 Moves location when directed without growling      Moves location when…  9    #>  7 Does not rush through doorways                     Does not rush throu…  8    #>  8 General social skills and obedience                (bold, highlight-FF…  7    #>  9 Can play or interact appropriately with other dogs Can play or interac…  7    #> 10 Can be groomed or handled without squirming        Can be groomed or h…  8    #> 11 Stops barking on command                           Stops barking on co…  6 example_spreadsheetb <- system.file("extdata/boutiques.xlsx", package = "unheadr") annotate_mf_all(example_spreadsheetb) #> # A tibble: 7 × 6 #>   `Store Location`                  Q1_2012      Q1_2013 Q1_2014 Q1_2015 Q1_2016 #>   <chr>                             <chr>        <chr>   <chr>   <chr>   <chr>   #> 1 (highlight-FFADC5E7) London       (highlight-… (bold,… (highl… (highl… (bold,… #> 2 (highlight-FFADC5E7) Paris        (highlight-… (highl… (highl… (bold,… (highl… #> 3 (highlight-FFF37B70) Atlanta      (bold, high… (highl… (highl… (highl… (highl… #> 4 (highlight-FFADC5E7) Madrid       (highlight-… (bold,… (bold,… (bold,… (highl… #> 5 (highlight-FFADC5E7) Rome         (highlight-… (highl… (highl… (highl… (highl… #> 6 (highlight-FFF37B70) Mexico City  (bold, high… (highl… (bold,… (highl… (bold,… #> 7 (highlight-FFADC5E7, italic) Rome (highlight-… (highl… (highl… (highl… (highl…

title: Introduction to unheadr
text: Tables PDF similar sources can often imported R character vectors one element line. can parsed fixed width files separated columns. unheadr now includes regex_valign() function aligning elements vectors vertically inserting padding whitespace (optional separators) positions along line matched regular expression. example based data hotel guests (ID, State Origin, Date) PDF parsed pdftools::pdf_text. inconsistent whitespace first second data ‘columns’. regular expression matches word boundary uppercase letters, can adjust whitespace matched positions across lines. output easier parse readr data-munging approaches.
code: guests <-    unlist(strsplit(c("6     COAHUILA        20/03/2020 712        COAHUILA             20/03/2020"),"\n"))  guests #> [1] "6     COAHUILA        20/03/2020"           #> [2] "712        COAHUILA             20/03/2020" regex_valign(guests, "\\b(?=[A-Z])") #> [1] "6          COAHUILA        20/03/2020"      #> [2] "712        COAHUILA             20/03/2020"

title: Handle Data with Messy Header Rows and Broken Values
text: Load package first.
code: library(unheadr)

title: Handle Data with Messy Header Rows and Broken Values
text: untangle2() untangle2() puts embedded subheaders variable, using regular expressions identify . data (subset bundled dataset can loaded data(primates2017)), rows correspond values grouping variables. column. Instead, embedded within data rectangle. common practice many disciplines. data presentation looks OK easy read, hard work (example: calculating group-wise summaries). example, values implicit “geographic region” variable implicit “taxonomic family” variable embedded column contains observational units (scientific names various primates). tidier structure, subheaders embedded scientific_name column need plucked placed variable. initially main objective unheadr untangle2() made . function can used magrittr pipes dplyr-type verb. subheaders can matched bulk regular expression share prefix, suffix, anything common, can save lot time. Otherwise, can matched name. details, see examples vignette. ‘untangled’ version data: Now can easily perform grouping operations summarize data (e.g. calculating average body mass Family). unbreak_vals() function uses regex fix values broken across two rows. usually happens formatting table need fit page. can match opening brackets regex. unwrap_cols() Use function unwrap glue values wrapped across multiple rows presentation purposes, inconsistent number empty NA values padding columns. Unwrap elements variable defines groups, separating commas. unbreak_rows() function merges sets two contiguous rows upwards pasting values lagging row values leading row (identified using regular expressions). following table basketball records two sets header rows values broken across two contiguous rows. unbreak_rows() merges rows can match common pattern. mash_colnames() column names broken across top n rows data frame tibble, mash_colnames() makes many header rows column names. Existing names can kept ignored. inputs ragged column names (NA values first row), first row can filled row-wise mashing. annotate_mf() annotate_mf_all() Sometimes embedded subheaders can’t matched content context, share formatting spreadsheet file. annotate_mf() flattens four common approaches confer meaningful formatting cells adds character string target variable. annotate_mf_all() applies approach values dataset. Lastly, regex_valign() can adjust whitespace (padding) within character vector one element per line, easier parsing readr. inconsistent whitespace elements line can adjusted matching position interest regular expressions.
code: # Set up a toy dataset dogsDesc <-   data.frame(     stringsAsFactors = FALSE,     dogs = c(       "Retriever", "(Golden)",       "Retriever", "(Labrador)", "Bulldog", "(French)"     ),     coat = c("long", NA, "short", NA, "short", NA)   )  dogsDesc #>         dogs  coat #> 1  Retriever  long #> 2   (Golden)  <NA> #> 3  Retriever short #> 4 (Labrador)  <NA> #> 5    Bulldog short #> 6   (French)  <NA> unbreak_vals(df = dogsDesc, regex = "^\\(", ogcol = dogs, newcol = dogs_unbroken) #>          dogs_unbroken  coat #> 1   Retriever (Golden)  long #> 2 Retriever (Labrador) short #> 3     Bulldog (French) short # Set up the data nyk <-   data.frame(     stringsAsFactors = FALSE,     player = c(       "Marcus Camby", NA, NA,       NA, NA, NA, NA, "Allan Houston", NA,       "Latrell Sprewell", NA, NA     ),     listed_height_m. = c(       2.11, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA,       NA, 1.98, NA, 1.96, NA, NA     ),     teams_chronological = c(       "Raptors", "Knicks",       "Nuggets", "Clippers", "Trail Blazers",       "Rockets", "Knicks", "Pistons",       "Knicks", "Warriors", "Knicks",       "Timberwolves"     ),     position = c(       "Power forward", "Center",       NA, NA, NA, NA, NA,       "Shooting guard", NA, "Small forward", NA, NA     )   ) nyk #>              player listed_height_m. teams_chronological       position #> 1      Marcus Camby             2.11             Raptors  Power forward #> 2              <NA>               NA              Knicks         Center #> 3              <NA>               NA             Nuggets           <NA> #> 4              <NA>               NA            Clippers           <NA> #> 5              <NA>               NA       Trail Blazers           <NA> #> 6              <NA>               NA             Rockets           <NA> #> 7              <NA>               NA              Knicks           <NA> #> 8     Allan Houston             1.98             Pistons Shooting guard #> 9              <NA>               NA              Knicks           <NA> #> 10 Latrell Sprewell             1.96            Warriors  Small forward #> 11             <NA>               NA              Knicks           <NA> #> 12             <NA>               NA        Timberwolves           <NA> unwrap_cols(nyk, groupingVar = player, separator = ", ") #> # A tibble: 3 x 4 #>   player     listed_height_m. teams_chronological                  position      #>   <chr>      <chr>            <chr>                                <chr>         #> 1 Marcus Ca… 2.11             Raptors, Knicks, Nuggets, Clippers,… Power forwar… #> 2 Allan Hou… 1.98             Pistons, Knicks                      Shooting gua… #> 3 Latrell S… 1.96             Warriors, Knicks, Timberwolves       Small forward bball <- data.frame(   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,   v1 = c(     "Player", NA, "Sleve McDichael", "Dean Wesrey",     "Karl Dandleton", "Player",     NA,     "Mike Sernandez",     "Glenallen Mixon",     "Rey McSriff"   ),   v2 = c(     "Most points", "in a game", "55", "43", "41", "Most varsity",     "games played", "111", "109",     "104"   ),   v3 = c(     "Season", "(year ending)", "2001", "2000", "2010", "Season",     "(year ending)", "2005",     "2004", "2002"   ) ) # Match with regex on variable v2 unbreak_rows(bball, regex = "^Most", ogcol = v2) #> 2 matches #>                v1                        v2                   v3 #> 1          Player     Most points in a game Season (year ending) #> 2 Sleve McDichael                        55                 2001 #> 3     Dean Wesrey                        43                 2000 #> 4  Karl Dandleton                        41                 2010 #> 5          Player Most varsity games played Season (year ending) #> 6  Mike Sernandez                       111                 2005 #> 7 Glenallen Mixon                       109                 2004 #> 8     Rey McSriff                       104                 2002 # Data with broken headers babies <-   data.frame(     stringsAsFactors = FALSE,     Baby = c(NA, NA, "Angie", "Yean", "Pierre"),     Age = c("in", "months", "11", "9", "7"),     Weight = c("kg", NA, "2", "3", "4"),     Ward = c(NA, NA, "A", "B", "C")   )  babies #>     Baby    Age Weight Ward #> 1   <NA>     in     kg <NA> #> 2   <NA> months   <NA> <NA> #> 3  Angie     11      2    A #> 4   Yean      9      3    B #> 5 Pierre      7      4    C # Mash, including the object names mash_colnames(babies, n_name_rows = 2, keep_names = TRUE) #>     Baby Age_in_months Weight_kg Ward #> 3  Angie            11         2    A #> 4   Yean             9         3    B #> 5 Pierre             7         4    C # Data with ragged headers survey <-   data.frame(     stringsAsFactors = FALSE,     X1 = c("Participant", NA, "12", "34", "45", "123"),     X2 = c(       "How did you hear about us?",       "TV", "TRUE", "FALSE", "FALSE", "FALSE"     ),     X3 = c(NA, "Social Media", "FALSE", "TRUE", "FALSE", "FALSE"),     X4 = c(NA, "Radio", "FALSE", "TRUE", "FALSE", "TRUE"),     X5 = c(NA, "Flyer", "FALSE", "FALSE", "FALSE", "FALSE"),     X6 = c("Age", NA, "31", "23", "19", "24")   ) survey #>            X1                         X2           X3    X4    X5   X6 #> 1 Participant How did you hear about us?         <NA>  <NA>  <NA>  Age #> 2        <NA>                         TV Social Media Radio Flyer <NA> #> 3          12                       TRUE        FALSE FALSE FALSE   31 #> 4          34                      FALSE         TRUE  TRUE FALSE   23 #> 5          45                      FALSE        FALSE FALSE FALSE   19 #> 6         123                      FALSE        FALSE  TRUE FALSE   24 # Ignoring names and using sliding headers mash_colnames(survey,2,keep_names = FALSE,sliding_headers = TRUE, sep = "_") #>   Participant How did you hear about us?_TV #> 3          12                          TRUE #> 4          34                         FALSE #> 5          45                         FALSE #> 6         123                         FALSE #>   How did you hear about us?_Social Media How did you hear about us?_Radio #> 3                                   FALSE                            FALSE #> 4                                    TRUE                             TRUE #> 5                                   FALSE                            FALSE #> 6                                   FALSE                             TRUE #>   How did you hear about us?_Flyer Age #> 3                            FALSE  31 #> 4                            FALSE  23 #> 5                            FALSE  19 #> 6                            FALSE  24 example_spreadsheet <- system.file("extdata/dog_test.xlsx", package = "unheadr") annotate_mf(example_spreadsheet,orig = Task, new=Task_annotated) example_spreadsheet_all <- system.file("extdata/boutiques.xlsx", package = "unheadr") annotate_mf(example_spreadsheet_all) guests <-    unlist(strsplit(c("6     COAHUILA        20/03/2020 712        COAHUILA             20/03/2020"),"\n")) guests #> [1] "6     COAHUILA        20/03/2020"           #> [2] "712        COAHUILA             20/03/2020" regex_valign(guests, "\\b(?=[A-Z])") #> [1] "6          COAHUILA        20/03/2020"      #> [2] "712        COAHUILA             20/03/2020"

title: Annotate meaningful formatting — annotate_mf

Ensure data spreadsheet rectangular running; includes blank formatted cells beyond data rectangle.

code: example_spreadsheet <- system.file("extdata/dog_test.xlsx", package = "unheadr") annotate_mf(example_spreadsheet, orig = Task, new = Task_annotated) #> # A tibble: 11 × 3 #>    Task                                               Task_annotated       Score #>    <chr>                                              <chr>                <dbl> #>  1 Outdoor activities                                 (bold, highlight-FF…  7.67 #>  2 Walks on a loose leash without pulling             Walks on a loose le…  7    #>  3 Walks without chasing bicycles, animals, etc.      Walks without chasi…  6    #>  4 Greets friends and strangers without jumping       Greets friends and … 10    #>  5 Home behavior                                      (bold, highlight-FF…  8.5  #>  6 Moves location when directed without growling      Moves location when…  9    #>  7 Does not rush through doorways                     Does not rush throu…  8    #>  8 General social skills and obedience                (bold, highlight-FF…  7    #>  9 Can play or interact appropriately with other dogs Can play or interac…  7    #> 10 Can be groomed or handled without squirming        Can be groomed or h…  8    #> 11 Stops barking on command                           Stops barking on co…  6

what: Make many header rows into column names — mash_colnames
title: Make many header rows into column names — mash_colnames
text: Make many header rows column names

title: Make many header rows into column names — mash_colnames
code: mash_colnames(   df,   n_name_rows,   keep_names = TRUE,   sliding_headers = FALSE,   sep = "_" )

title: Make many header rows into column names — mash_colnames
text: df data.frame tibble object names broken across top n rows. n_name_rows Number rows top data used create new variable (column) names. Must >= 1. keep_names TRUE, existing names included building new variable names. Defaults TRUE. sliding_headers TRUE, empty values first (topmost) header header row filled column-wise. Defaults FALSE. See details. sep Character string separate unified values (default underscore).

title: Make many header rows into column names — mash_colnames
text: original data frame, new column names without top n rows held broken names.

title: Make many header rows into column names — mash_colnames
text: Tables often shared column names broken across first rows. function takes number rows top table hold broken names whether include names, mashes values column-wise single string column. keep_names argument can helpful tables imported using skip argument. keep_names set FALSE, adjust value n_name_rows accordingly. function throw warning possible NA values end variable names. sliding_headers can used tables ragged names every column value first row. cases attribution adjacency assumed, sliding_headers set TRUE names topmost row filled row-wise. can useful tables reporting survey data experimental designs untidy manner.

title: Make many header rows into column names — mash_colnames
text: function originally contributed Jarrett Byrnes GitHub issue.

title: Make many header rows into column names — mash_colnames
code: babies <-   data.frame(     stringsAsFactors = FALSE,     Baby = c(NA, NA, "Angie", "Yean", "Pierre"),     Age = c("in", "months", "11", "9", "7"),     Weight = c("kg", NA, "2", "3", "4"),     Ward = c(NA, NA, "A", "B", "C")   ) # Including the object names mash_colnames(babies, n_name_rows = 2, keep_names = TRUE) #>     Baby Age_in_months Weight_kg Ward #> 3  Angie            11         2    A #> 4   Yean             9         3    B #> 5 Pierre             7         4    C  babies_skip <-   data.frame(     stringsAsFactors = FALSE,     X1 = c("Baby", NA, NA, "Jennie", "Yean", "Pierre"),     X2 = c("Age", "in", "months", "11", "9", "7"),     X3 = c("Hospital", NA, NA, "A", "B", "A")   ) #' # Discarding the automatically-generated names (X1, X2, etc...) mash_colnames(babies_skip, n_name_rows = 3, keep_names = FALSE) #>     Baby Age_in_months Hospital #> 4 Jennie            11        A #> 5   Yean             9        B #> 6 Pierre             7        A  fish_experiment <-   data.frame(     stringsAsFactors = FALSE,     X1 = c("Sample", NA, "Pacific", "Atlantic", "Freshwater"),     X2 = c("Larvae", "Control", "12", "11", "10"),     X3 = c(NA, "Low Dose", "11", "12", "8"),     X4 = c(NA, "High Dose", "8", "7", "9"),     X5 = c("Adult", "Control", "13", "13", "8"),     X6 = c(NA, "Low Dose", "13", "12", "7"),     X7 = c(NA, "High Dose", "10", "10", "9")   ) # Ragged names mash_colnames(fish_experiment,   n_name_rows = 2,   keep_names = FALSE, sliding_headers = TRUE ) #>       Sample Larvae_Control Larvae_Low Dose Larvae_High Dose Adult_Control #> 3    Pacific             12              11                8            13 #> 4   Atlantic             11              12                7            13 #> 5 Freshwater             10               8                9             8 #>   Adult_Low Dose Adult_High Dose #> 3             13              10 #> 4             12              10 #> 5              7               9

what: Vertical character string alignment through regular expressions — regex_valign
title: Vertical character string alignment through regular expressions — regex_valign
text: Aligning strings regex.

title: Vertical character string alignment through regular expressions — regex_valign
code: regex_valign(stringvec, regex_ai, sep_str = "")

title: Vertical character string alignment through regular expressions — regex_valign
text: stringvec character vector one element line. regex_ai regular expression matching position alignment. sep_str Optional character vector inserted positions matched regular expression.

title: Vertical character string alignment through regular expressions — regex_valign
text: character vector one element line, padding inserted matched positions elements vertically aligned across lines.

title: Vertical character string alignment through regular expressions — regex_valign
text: Written mainly reading fixed width files, text, tables parsed PDFs.

title: Vertical character string alignment through regular expressions — regex_valign
code: guests <-   unlist(strsplit(c("6       COAHUILA        20/03/2020 7       COAHUILA             20/03/2020 18 BAJA CALIFORNIA     16/03/2020 109       CDMX      12/03/2020 1230   QUERETARO       21/03/2020"), "\n"))  # align at first uppercase word boundary , inserting a separator regex_valign(guests, "\\b(?=[A-Z])", " - ") #> [1] "6          - COAHUILA        20/03/2020"      #> [2] "7          - COAHUILA             20/03/2020" #> [3] "18         - BAJA CALIFORNIA     16/03/2020"  #> [4] "109        - CDMX      12/03/2020"            #> [5] "1230       - QUERETARO       21/03/2020"      # align dates at end of string regex_valign(guests, "\\b(?=[0-9]{2}[\\/]{1}[0-9]{2}[\\/]{1}[0-9]{4}$)") #> [1] "6       COAHUILA             20/03/2020" #> [2] "7       COAHUILA             20/03/2020" #> [3] "18 BAJA CALIFORNIA           16/03/2020" #> [4] "109       CDMX               12/03/2020" #> [5] "1230   QUERETARO             21/03/2020"

vecWithNewlines <- c("dog

cat


pig
") squish_newlines(vecWithNewlines) #> [1] "dog
cat
pig"

title: Merge rows up — unbreak_rows
text: Merge rows

title: Merge rows up — unbreak_rows
code: unbreak_rows(df, regex, ogcol, sep = " ")

title: Merge rows up — unbreak_rows
text: df data frame least two contiguous rows merged. regex regular expression identify sets rows merged, meant leading two contiguous rows. ogcol Variable text strings match. sep Character string separate unified values (default space).

title: Merge rows up — unbreak_rows
text: tibble data frame merged rows. Values lagging rows pasted onto values leading row, whitespace squished, lagging row dropped.

title: Merge rows up — unbreak_rows
text: function recodes empty strings ("") NA smoother pattern matching.

title: Merge rows up — unbreak_rows
code: bball <-   data.frame(     stringsAsFactors = FALSE,     v1 = c(       "Player", NA, "Sleve McDichael", "Dean Wesrey",       "Karl Dandleton"     ),     v2 = c("Most points", "in a game", "55", "43", "41"),     v3 = c("Season", "(year ending)", "2001", "2000", "2010")   ) unbreak_rows(bball, "Most", v2) #> 1 match #>                v1                    v2                   v3 #> 1          Player Most points in a game Season (year ending) #> 2 Sleve McDichael                    55                 2001 #> 3     Dean Wesrey                    43                 2000 #> 4  Karl Dandleton                    41                 2010

what: Unbreak values using regex to match the lagging half of the broken value — unbreak_vals

title: Unbreak values using regex to match the lagging half of the broken value — unbreak_vals
text: df data frame one values within variable broken across two rows. regex Regular expression matching trailing (lagging) half broken values. ogcol Variable unbreak. newcol Name new variable unified values. sep Character string separate unified values (default space). slice_groups Deprecated. See details Package News.

title: Unbreak values using regex to match the lagging half of the broken value — unbreak_vals
text: tibble 'unbroken' values. variable originally contained broken values gets dropped, new variable unified values placed first column. slice_groups argument now deprecated; extra rows variable broken values dropped.

title: Unbreak values using regex to match the lagging half of the broken value — unbreak_vals
text: function limited quite specific cases, useful dealing tables contain, example, scientific names broken across two rows. unwrapping values, see unwrap_cols.

title: Unbreak values using regex to match the lagging half of the broken value — unbreak_vals
code: data(primates2017_broken) # regex matches strings starting in lowercase (broken species epithets) unbreak_vals(primates2017_broken, "^[a-z]", scientific_name, sciname_new) #> # A tibble: 16 × 4 #>    sciname_new                  common_name              red_list_status mass_kg #>    <chr>                        <chr>                    <chr>           <chr>   #>  1 Semnopithecus johnii         Nilgiri Langur           VU              11.45   #>  2 Trachypithecus obscurus      Dusky Langur             NT              7.13    #>  3 Presbytis sumatra            Black Sumatran Langur    EN              6       #>  4 Trachypithecus auratus       East Javan Langur        VU              6.25    #>  5 Trachypithecus delacouri     Delacour's Langur        CR              NA      #>  6 Trachypithecus leucocephalus White-headed Langur      CR              8       #>  7 Presbytis comata             Javan Langur             EN              6.7     #>  8 Macaca pagensis              Pagai Macaque            CR              4.5     #>  9 Trachypithecus germaini      Germain's Langur         EN              8.83    #> 10 Macaca munzala               Arunachal Macaque        EN              NA      #> 11 Macaca mulatta               Rhesus Macaque           LC              9.9     #> 12 Semnopithecus hector         Terai Sacred Langur      NT              15.2    #> 13 Hylobates klossii            Kloss's Gibbon           EN              5.8     #> 14 Nycticebus menagensis        Philippine Slow Loris    VU              0.28    #> 15 Nycticebus bengalensis       Bengal Slow Loris        VU              1.21    #> 16 Nomascus concolor            Western Black Crested G… CR              7.71

what: unheadr: Handle Data with Messy Header Rows and Broken Values — unheadr-package
title: unheadr: Handle Data with Messy Header Rows and Broken Values — unheadr-package
text: Verb-like functions work messy data, often derived spreadsheets parsed PDF tables. Includes functions unwrapping values broken across rows, relocating embedded grouping values, annotate meaningful formatting spreadsheet files.

title: unheadr: Handle Data with Messy Header Rows and Broken Values — unheadr-package
text: Maintainer: Luis D. Verde Arregoitia luis@liomys.mx (ORCID)

title: Rectangling embedded subheaders — untangle2
text: Rectangling embedded subheaders

title: Rectangling embedded subheaders — untangle2
code: untangle2(df, regex, orig, new)

title: Rectangling embedded subheaders — untangle2
text: df data frame embedded subheaders. regex Regular expression match subheaders. orig Variable containing extraneous subheaders. new Name variable contain group values.

title: Rectangling embedded subheaders — untangle2
text: tibble without matched subheaders new variable containing grouping data.

title: Rectangling embedded subheaders — untangle2
text: Special thanks Jenny Bryan fixing initial tidyeval code overall function structure.

title: Rectangling embedded subheaders — untangle2
code: data(primates2017) # put taxonomic family in its own variable (matches the suffix "DAE") untangle2(primates2017, "DAE$", scientific_name, family) #> 11 matches #> # A tibble: 58 × 5 #>    scientific_name              common_name       red_list_status mass_kg family #>    <chr>                        <chr>             <chr>           <chr>   <chr>  #>  1 Asia                         NA                NA              NA      NA     #>  2 Trachypithecus obscurus      Dusky Langur      NT              7.13    CERCO… #>  3 Presbytis sumatra            Black Sumatran L… EN              6       CERCO… #>  4 Rhinopithecus roxellana      Golden Snub-nose… EN              NA      CERCO… #>  5 Trachypithecus auratus       East Javan Langur VU              6.25    CERCO… #>  6 Semnopithecus johnii         Nilgiri Langur    VU              11.45   CERCO… #>  7 Trachypithecus delacouri     Delacour's Langur CR              NA      CERCO… #>  8 Trachypithecus leucocephalus White-headed Lan… CR              8       CERCO… #>  9 Presbytis comata             Javan Langur      EN              6.7     CERCO… #> 10 Macaca pagensis              Pagai Macaque     CR              4.5     CERCO… #> # ℹ 48 more rows # put geographic regions in their own variable (matching them all by name) untangle2(   primates2017, "Asia|Madagascar|Mainland Africa|Neotropics",   scientific_name, family ) #> 4 matches #> # A tibble: 65 × 5 #>    scientific_name              common_name       red_list_status mass_kg family #>    <chr>                        <chr>             <chr>           <chr>   <chr>  #>  1 CERCOPITHECIDAE              NA                NA              NA      Asia   #>  2 Trachypithecus obscurus      Dusky Langur      NT              7.13    Asia   #>  3 Presbytis sumatra            Black Sumatran L… EN              6       Asia   #>  4 Rhinopithecus roxellana      Golden Snub-nose… EN              NA      Asia   #>  5 Trachypithecus auratus       East Javan Langur VU              6.25    Asia   #>  6 Semnopithecus johnii         Nilgiri Langur    VU              11.45   Asia   #>  7 Trachypithecus delacouri     Delacour's Langur CR              NA      Asia   #>  8 Trachypithecus leucocephalus White-headed Lan… CR              8       Asia   #>  9 Presbytis comata             Javan Langur      EN              6.7     Asia   #> 10 Macaca pagensis              Pagai Macaque     CR              4.5     Asia   #> # ℹ 55 more rows # with magrittr pipes (re-exported in this package) primates2017 %>%   untangle2("DAE$", scientific_name, family) %>%   untangle2(     "Asia|Madagascar|Mainland Africa|Neotropics",     scientific_name, region   ) #> 11 matches #> 4 matches #> # A tibble: 54 × 6 #>    scientific_name             common_name red_list_status mass_kg family region #>    <chr>                       <chr>       <chr>           <chr>   <chr>  <chr>  #>  1 Trachypithecus obscurus     Dusky Lang… NT              7.13    CERCO… Asia   #>  2 Presbytis sumatra           Black Suma… EN              6       CERCO… Asia   #>  3 Rhinopithecus roxellana     Golden Snu… EN              NA      CERCO… Asia   #>  4 Trachypithecus auratus      East Javan… VU              6.25    CERCO… Asia   #>  5 Semnopithecus johnii        Nilgiri La… VU              11.45   CERCO… Asia   #>  6 Trachypithecus delacouri    Delacour's… CR              NA      CERCO… Asia   #>  7 Trachypithecus leucocephal… White-head… CR              8       CERCO… Asia   #>  8 Presbytis comata            Javan Lang… EN              6.7     CERCO… Asia   #>  9 Macaca pagensis             Pagai Maca… CR              4.5     CERCO… Asia   #> 10 Trachypithecus germaini     Germain's … EN              8.83    CERCO… Asia   #> # ℹ 44 more rows

what: Unwrap values and clean up NAs used as padding — unwrap_cols
title: Unwrap values and clean up NAs used as padding — unwrap_cols
text: Unwrap values clean NAs used padding

title: Unwrap values and clean up NAs used as padding — unwrap_cols
code: unwrap_cols(df, groupingVar, separator)

title: Unwrap values and clean up NAs used as padding — unwrap_cols
text: df data frame wrapped values inconsistent number NA values used within-group padding. groupingVar Name variable describing observational units. separator Character string defining separator delimit elements unwrapped value.

title: Unwrap values and clean up NAs used as padding — unwrap_cols
text: summarized tibble. Order preserved grouping variable making factor.

title: Unwrap values and clean up NAs used as padding — unwrap_cols
text: roughly opposite tidyr::separate_rows().

title: Unwrap values and clean up NAs used as padding — unwrap_cols
code: data(primates2017_wrapped) # using commas to separate elements unwrap_cols(primates2017_wrapped, scientific_name, ", ") #> # A tibble: 2 × 6 #>   scientific_name         common_name    habitat red_list_status mass_kg country #>   <chr>                   <chr>          <chr>   <chr>           <chr>   <chr>   #> 1 Trachypithecus germaini Germain's Lan… evergr… EN              8.83    Cambod… #> 2 Nycticebus menagensis   Philippine Sl… lowlan… VU              0.28    Brunei…  # separating with semicolons df <- data.frame(   ounits = c("A", NA, "B", "C", "D", NA),   vals = c(1, 2, 2, 3, 1, 3) ) unwrap_cols(df, ounits, ";") #> # A tibble: 4 × 2 #>   ounits vals  #>   <chr>  <chr> #> 1 A      1;2   #> 2 B      2     #> 3 C      3     #> 4 D      1;3

title: unheadr 0.1.0
text: Added NEWS.md file track changes package. Added .slice_groups argument unbreak_vals either keep discard variable broken values slice extra rows afterwards. Reworded documentation adequate terms (.e. embedded subheaders).

title: unheadr 0.1.1
text: Added tests, spreadsheet-related functions, better documentation.

title: unheadr 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2020-02-09 Cleanup preparation release.

title: unheadr 0.2.1
text: CRAN release: 2020-03-04 Minor patch address breaking changes planned tibble.

title: unheadr 0.2.2
text: Adds annotate_mf_all() function translate meaningful formatting cells spreadsheet. Adds regex_valign() function aligning character strings one element line.

title: unheadr 0.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2020-10-25 Adds mash_colnames make many header rows column names. Drops leading dot notation unbreak_vals _slice/__groups_ argument ultimately deprecates argument. Two datasets added teaching demonstration purposes.

title: unheadr 0.3.1
text: CRAN release: 2021-02-06 informative warning messages mash_colnames Re-converted character columns new datasets.

title: unheadr 0.3.2
text: CRAN release: 2021-04-09 Fixed issue mash_colnames broke output separator underscore. Improve wording documentation unbreaking trailing halves broken values.

title: unheadr 0.3.3
text: CRAN release: 2022-08-15 Documentation fix HTML5 checks
